title: Topic 449: Why E3 Mattered: The Psychology of Gaming Events
content:
# Topic 449: Why E3 Mattered: The Psychology of Gaming Events

## ABSTRACT

This paper explores the cultural phenomenon of the Electronic Entertainment Expo (E3), an annual trade show that, for a quarter-century, was the Mecca for video game enthusiasts, journalists, and developers. We argue that E3 was more than just a marketing event; it was a ritualistic pilgrimage that tapped into deep-seated psychological needs for community, spectacle, and the collective effervescence of shared hype. Through a rigorous, albeit tongue-in-cheek, analysis of E3’s history, its most iconic moments, and its eventual demise, we reveal how the event’s psychological pull shaped the gaming industry and consumer behavior. We also mourn the loss of a time when game announcements weren’t just tweeted out on a random Tuesday, but were delivered with the pomp and circumstance of a royal decree, often with a healthy dose of awkward celebrity endorsements.

## INTRODUCTION

Once upon a time, in a magical land called Los Angeles, there existed a sacred annual ritual known as the Electronic Entertainment Expo, or E3. For three days every year, the video game industry would descend upon the Los Angeles Convention Center, transforming it into a veritable Nerdvana. It was a place where grown adults would queue for hours to play a 10-minute demo of a game that was still a year away from release, where the collective gasp of a thousand journalists at a surprise announcement could register on the Richter scale, and where the future of interactive entertainment was revealed, one over-the-top press conference at a time.

E3 was, for all intents and purposes, the Christmas of video games. It was a time of hope, of anticipation, and of the kind of unadulterated, childlike joy that only a new Zelda trailer can inspire. But it was also a time of crushing disappointment, of broken promises, and of the soul-crushing realization that the game you’ve been hyped for all year is, in fact, a buggy mess. This paper is not a history of E3, though it contains history. It is not a lament for its passing, though it contains lamentation. It is, instead, a psychological autopsy of a cultural phenomenon. We will dissect the hype, the spectacle, and the collective delusion that made E3 so special. We will explore why, in an age of instant gratification and on-demand everything, we still crave the shared experience of a live event. And we will ask the most important question of all: was it really about the games, or was it about the friends we made along the way (and the free swag)?

## LITERATURE REVIEW

To understand the psychological grip of E3, one must first wade through the hallowed, if somewhat dusty, halls of academia. The literature on hype, it turns out, is as vast and varied as the line for the Nintendo booth in 2004. Scholars have long been fascinated by the human tendency to get unreasonably excited about things that don’t exist yet. This phenomenon, which we in the biz call “hype culture,” is the engine that powered E3. It’s a potent cocktail of marketing savvy, social contagion, and a fundamental human need for something to look forward to, other than the sweet release of death [1].

At the heart of the E3 experience was the psychological concept of **collective effervescence**, a term coined by sociologist Émile Durkheim to describe the shared feeling of identity and excitement that emerges from a group ritual [2]. Durkheim, who was clearly a man who knew how to party, argued that these moments of collective assembly are essential for social cohesion. E3, with its roaring crowds, thunderous music, and on-stage pyrotechnics, was a textbook example of a modern-day ritual. It was a place where thousands of individuals, united by their love of video games, could come together and experience a sense of belonging that transcended their everyday lives. It was, in essence, a church for people who worship at the altar of Hideo Kojima.

Of course, E3 was also a trade show, and the literature on consumer behavior at such events is equally illuminating. Studies have shown that the sensory overload of a trade show floor—the flashing lights, the blaring music, the scantily clad booth babes (a practice that, thankfully, has largely been consigned to the dustbin of history)—can have a profound effect on our decision-making processes [3]. This “sensory branding” is designed to bypass our rational minds and appeal directly to our primal instincts. It’s the reason why, after three days at E3, you’d find yourself inexplicably excited about a game about a goat simulator. You weren’t just buying a game; you were buying into the experience, the community, and the collective delusion that this, finally, would be the game that would change your life.

Finally, we must consider the role of **social identity theory** in the E3 phenomenon. This theory posits that our sense of self is derived, in part, from our membership in social groups [4]. For many gamers, E3 was a physical manifestation of their online communities. It was a place where they could meet their virtual friends in the flesh, bond over their shared passions, and reinforce their identity as “gamers.” This sense of belonging is a powerful motivator, and it’s one of the reasons why people were willing to spend thousands of dollars to attend E3, even when they could have watched the press conferences for free at home in their underwear. It wasn’t just about the games; it was about being part of the tribe.

## ANALYSIS

The psychological power of E3 can be broken down into a few key components, each of which preys on our feeble human minds with the precision of a well-aimed headshot. First and foremost is the **hype cycle**, a rollercoaster of emotion that would make even the most seasoned therapist’s head spin. The cycle begins with the faintest of rumors, a leaked screenshot here, a cryptic tweet there. This is the “whisper phase,” where anticipation is a low hum, a background radiation of excitement. Then comes the official announcement, the grand reveal at an E3 press conference. This is the “peak of inflated expectations,” a moment of pure, unadulterated ecstasy where anything seems possible. The game is a masterpiece, a genre-defining classic, the second coming of Christ in digital form. The crowd roars, the internet explodes, and for a brief, shining moment, all is right with the world.

Of course, what goes up must come down. The peak is inevitably followed by the “trough of disillusionment,” as the harsh light of reality begins to creep in. The gameplay demo was a bit janky, the graphics weren’t quite as good as the pre-rendered trailer, and the developers have suddenly gone very quiet. This is the phase of doubt, of skepticism, of the dawning realization that you may have been had. But then, just as you’re about to give up hope, the cycle begins anew. A new trailer is released, a new feature is announced, and suddenly you’re right back on the hype train, destination: disappointment.

E3 was the master of the hype cycle, a finely tuned machine for generating and sustaining excitement. The press conferences were the main events, elaborate stage shows designed to maximize spectacle and surprise. The “one more thing” moment, a classic of the genre, was a masterclass in psychological manipulation. Just when you thought the show was over, a beloved developer would walk on stage to announce a long-awaited sequel or a brand-new IP. The resulting explosion of joy was a powerful drug, a hit of pure, uncut dopamine that would keep you buzzing for days. It’s the same principle that makes surprise parties so effective, the sudden, unexpected release of positive emotion that creates a lasting memory.

And then there was the queuing. Oh, the queuing. For the uninitiated, the idea of waiting in line for hours to play a 10-minute demo might seem like a form of self-torture. But for the E3 faithful, it was an essential part of the experience. The queue was a place of community, a temporary village where strangers became friends, united by their shared suffering and their love of video games. It was a place to trade stories, to speculate about the games, and to complain about the heat. The shared ordeal of the queue created a sense of camaraderie, a bond forged in the fires of boredom and dehydration. And when you finally reached the front of the line, the sense of relief and accomplishment was immense. You had earned this. You had suffered for your art. And the game, when you finally got to play it, was all the sweeter for it. This is the **IKEA effect** in action, the psychological principle that we place a higher value on things that we have put effort into creating, or in this case, waiting for.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The annals of E3 are littered with moments that perfectly encapsulate the psychological phenomena we’ve discussed. Take, for instance, the legendary E3 of 1995. Sega had just announced that its new console, the Saturn, would be available for the low, low price of $399. A respectable, if not exactly revolutionary, price point. Then, in a move that would go down in gaming history, Sony’s Steve Race walked on stage, uttered a single word—“$299”—and walked off. The crowd went wild. The internet, in its nascent form, exploded. In that one moment, the console war was won. It was a masterstroke of psychological warfare, a perfectly timed blow that left Sega reeling and established the PlayStation as the dominant force in the industry.

Then there was the E3 of 2004, a year that will forever be remembered for the reveal of *The Legend of Zelda: Twilight Princess*. The lights dimmed, the iconic music swelled, and a trailer began to play, showing a more mature, realistic take on the beloved franchise. The crowd was in a frenzy. When Shigeru Miyamoto, the creator of Zelda, appeared on stage wielding a sword and shield, the collective effervescence in the room reached a fever pitch. Grown men wept. It was a moment of pure, unadulterated joy, a reminder of the power of video games to transport us back to our childhoods.

Of course, not all E3 moments were so glorious. The 2010 Konami press conference is a case study in what happens when the hype train goes off the rails. A series of bizarre, cringeworthy presentations, including a particularly awkward demonstration of a wrestling game, left the audience bewildered and the internet in stitches. It was a reminder that, for all its spectacle, E3 was still a marketing event, and sometimes, the marketing just doesn’t land. The resulting memes and parodies were a form of collective catharsis, a way for the community to process the secondhand embarrassment of it all.

And who could forget the E3 of 2015, the so-called “Year of Dreams”? In a single press conference, Sony announced the return of *The Last Guardian*, a game that had been in development hell for nearly a decade; a full remake of the beloved classic *Final Fantasy VII*; and the resurrection of the long-dormant *Shenmue* franchise, funded by a Kickstarter campaign that was launched live on stage. It was a trifecta of fan service, a direct appeal to the nostalgia and wishful thinking of a generation of gamers. The reaction was ecstatic. It was a reminder that, even in an age of cynicism and skepticism, we still want to believe in the magic of video games.

## DISCUSSION

So what does all this tell us about ourselves? What can the rise and fall of a video game trade show reveal about the human condition? For one, it tells us that we are, at our core, social creatures. We crave connection, community, and the shared experience of a collective ritual. In an increasingly fragmented and individualized world, events like E3 provided a much-needed sense of belonging. They were a reminder that we are not alone in our passions, that there are others out there who share our love for this strange and wonderful hobby.

It also tells us that we are suckers for a good story. The hype cycle, for all its absurdity, is a form of storytelling. It’s a narrative of hope, of anticipation, of the promise of a better future. We buy into the hype not just because we want to play the game, but because we want to be part of the story. We want to be there when the next big thing is revealed, to be able to say, “I was there when…” It’s the same impulse that drives us to watch the Super Bowl, to follow the latest celebrity gossip, or to obsess over the stock market. We are all, in our own way, looking for a narrative to invest in.

Finally, the story of E3 is a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked hype. The pressure to deliver bigger and better spectacles each year led to a culture of over-promising and under-delivering. The infamous “E3 demo” became a running joke in the industry, a vertically sliced, heavily scripted version of a game that often bore little resemblance to the final product. This, in turn, led to a growing sense of cynicism and distrust among consumers. The magic of E3 began to fade, not because the games got worse, but because we, the audience, got wise to the tricks. The death of E3, then, can be seen as a kind of collective coming-of-age for the gaming community. We no longer need the spectacle, the hype, the validation of a live event. We have the internet now, a 24/7 hype machine that is far more efficient, if far less romantic, than a three-day trade show in Los Angeles.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, E3 mattered because we *believed* it mattered. It was a self-fulfilling prophecy, a collective hallucination that we all agreed to indulge in for a few days every year. It was a testament to the power of shared experience, a reminder that even in a world of digital isolation, there is still a deep human need for physical assembly. We may have come for the games, but we stayed for the community, the spectacle, and the intoxicating thrill of the hype.

E3 is dead. Long live E3. We may never see its like again, and perhaps that’s for the best. The industry has moved on, the world has changed, and we, the gamers, have grown up. But for a brief, shining moment, it was glorious. It was a time of magic, of wonder, and of the kind of unbridled optimism that can only come from a room full of people who truly believe in the power of video games to change the world. And for that, we will always be grateful. Now, if you’ll excuse me, I have to go watch a 24-hour livestream of a man in a chicken suit playing a kazoo. The future is now.
references:
[1] Locke's Journey. (2015, October 13). The Psychology of Hype and Pre-Order Culture. Retrieved from https://lockesjourney.wordpress.com/2015/10/13/the-psychology-of-hype-and-pre-order-culture/

[2] Durkheim, É. (1912). *The Elementary Forms of the Religious Life*.

[3] Exponents. (n.d.). Psychology of Customer Behavior in Trade Shows. Retrieved from https://www.exponents.com/our-blog/role-of-customer-behavior-in-creating-successful-trade-shows/

[4] Tajfel, H., & Turner, J. C. (1979). An integrative theory of intergroup conflict. In W. G. Austin & S. Worchel (Eds.), *The social psychology of intergroup relations* (pp. 33-47). Monterey, CA: Brooks/Cole.
